task: I’ve got a long edit with a hard delivery date, and the schedule is already getting squeezed by a couple of revisions. Can you help me sketch a realistic plan for the next few days so I can stay on track without burning the whole night on the wrong things?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the key is to **protect the final delivery path** and stop revisions from eating the whole schedule.

## Simple plan for the next few days

### 1) Lock the current baseline
- Freeze the current version as your working reference
- Make one clean duplicate for revisions
- Don’t keep branching into multiple “almost final” cuts

### 2) Sort revisions by impact
Split the notes into:
- **must-do for delivery**
- **nice-to-have if time allows**
- **needs client clarification**

Focus first on anything that affects:
- story clarity
- legal text
- audio sync
- export/spec compliance

### 3) Set a revision cutoff
- Decide when the next revision round ends
- Tell the client what can realistically make it into the next pass
- Avoid open-ended “one more tweak” loops

### 4) Build in a QC window
Before final export, leave time for:
- full watch-through
- audio check
- export spot-check
- file naming / version check

### 5) Protect a delivery buffer
- Stop making editorial changes before the deadline day if possible
- Use the last window for fixes, exports, and re-exports only
- Keep one backup export in case the first one fails

### 6) Communicate early
If the schedule is tightening, send a short update:
> Hi [Client Name] — I’m working through the current revision round and tightening the remaining deliverables against the deadline. I’m prioritizing the items that affect final delivery first, and I’ll confirm what’s making it into the next pass once I’ve finished the current round.

## Quick rule
Don’t spend the late hours on anything that won’t survive the final delivery.

If you want, I can turn this into a **day-by-day plan for the next 3 days** or a **priority list you can use against client notes**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is the right shape, but it’s still a little generic.

I’d want a real day-by-day split — like:
- today: lock picture / sort notes / identify blockers
- tomorrow: only the must-do revisions
- last day: QC, exports, checksum, upload, buffer

And I need the revision handling a bit sharper. Not just “must-do vs nice-to-have” — more like which notes are actually dangerous to ignore, which ones can wait, and what gets kicked back to the client because it needs a decision.

If you can map it to a 3-day schedule with actual work blocks and a cutoff for client changes, that’d be useful.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a more concrete **3-day plan** with a sharper revision split.

## Day 1: lock picture, sort notes, identify blockers
**Goal:** stop the cut from drifting.

### Work block 1
- Freeze the current version
- Duplicate the sequence/project
- Decide which cut is the working baseline

### Work block 2
Sort every note into:
- **dangerous to ignore** — affects story clarity, legal text, sync, missing content, export/spec
- **can wait** — polish, preference, minor wording, cosmetic tweaks
- **needs client decision** — conflicting notes, unclear direction, scope creep, new asks

### Work block 3
- Flag blockers early
- Separate revision notes from actual deliverables
- Send one short client message if anything is unclear:
  > Hi [Client Name] — I’m working through the current revision round and separating confirmed changes from open items. A few notes need a decision before I can move forward cleanly, so I’ll come back with those specifically rather than guessing.

### End of day cutoff
- No new creative branches
- No “while I’m in here” edits
- Only log new notes and clarify blockers

---

## Day 2: only must-do revisions
**Goal:** finish the edits that protect delivery.

### Work block 1
Handle only the **dangerous to ignore** notes:
- story clarity
- legal text
- missing shots
- sync issues
- audio problems
- export/spec issues

### Work block 2
- Make one pass through the full timeline after those fixes
- Don’t chase cosmetic polish unless it’s directly visible or client-critical
- If a note changes scope, move it to **needs client decision**

### Work block 3
- Update the change log
- Confirm what is now locked
- Send a short update if needed:
  > Hi [Client Name] — I’ve completed the items that affect the final delivery path. A few lower-priority notes remain, but I’m holding those unless you want them included in this version.

### Hard cutoff for client changes
- Set a specific time when new notes stop for this round
- Anything after that becomes a next-version item unless it’s a true delivery blocker

---

## Day 3: QC, exports, checksum, upload, buffer
**Goal:** get the file out cleanly and leave room for failure.

### Work block 1: QC
- Watch the cut start to finish once
- Check picture, audio, titles, legal, and final timing
- Confirm no temp elements are left in the export path

### Work block 2: exports
- Export final deliverables
- Check runtime, naming, codec, frame rate, resolution, aspect
- Spot-check the actual export, not just the timeline

### Work block 3: delivery checks
- Run checksum if required
- Verify upload completed correctly
- Confirm file opens after upload
- Keep one backup export ready

### Buffer
- Leave time for a re-export if anything fails
- Don’t plan to finish at the exact deadline

---

## Revision triage rule
Use this split:

### Dangerous to ignore
These can break delivery or create client pain:
- missing content
- wrong version
- sync issues
- audio problems
- legal text
- export/spec mismatches
- obvious continuity breaks

### Can wait
These are usually polish:
- tiny pacing tweaks
- cosmetic cleanup
- minor wording preferences
- non-essential visual refinements

### Needs client decision
These should not be guessed:
- conflicting notes
- new scope
- unclear preferences
- requests that contradict prior approvals

---

## Simple cutoff rule
After the Day 2 cutoff:
- only delivery blockers get in
- no new creative requests unless the client explicitly reopens scope
- anything unclear gets parked, not guessed

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean 3-day checklist with time blocks** like:
- morning / afternoon / end of day
- plus a short client update you can send on Day 1.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. Still a little too neat on paper though.

What I’d want is the actual block structure, not just the headings. Like:

**Day 1**
- 9–11: freeze picture, duplicate project, gather notes
- 11–1: triage notes into dangerous / wait / decision
- 2–4: clear blockers, send client questions
- 4:30 cutoff: no more creative changes, only logging and prep

**Day 2**
- morning: must-do revisions only
- afternoon: pass for anything that changed downstream
- end of day: lock the cut, send status

**Day 3**
- morning: full QC watch
- midday: exports / spot-checks / checksum
- afternoon: upload, verify, buffer for re-export

And on the revision split, I’d separate it a bit more aggressively:
- **dangerous to ignore** = anything that can fail QC, break continuity, or cause a client to reject the master
- **can wait** = polish, preferences, micro-timing, cosmetic stuff
- **kick back for decision** = anything that changes scope, conflicts with prior approvals, or needs them to choose between two options

If you want to make it actually useful, give me the 3-day version in that format with a hard client-note cutoff time and one short update email for the end of Day 1.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the more operational 3-day version.

## 3-day plan

### Day 1 — freeze, triage, unblock
**9–11**  
- Freeze picture
- Duplicate the project / sequence
- Gather all notes in one place
- Mark the current version as the working baseline

**11–1**  
- Triage every note into:
  - **dangerous to ignore**
  - **can wait**
  - **kick back for decision**

**2–4**  
- Clear blockers only
- Ask the client about anything unclear or conflicting
- Do not start polish passes yet

**4:30 cutoff**  
- No more creative changes
- Only logging, prep, and note cleanup after this point

**End of day**
- Send a short status update

> Hi [Client Name] — I’ve frozen the current version and sorted the remaining notes. I’m moving first on the items that affect delivery, and I have a few points that need your decision before I can finish the next pass cleanly. I’ll send those separately so I don’t guess on anything important.

---

### Day 2 — must-do revisions only
**Morning**  
- Work only on **dangerous to ignore** items:
  - QC failures
  - continuity breaks
  - missing content
  - legal text
  - sync/audio issues
  - export/spec problems

**Afternoon**  
- Do one downstream pass on anything that changed
- Check titles, audio, timing, and any affected exports
- Do not chase polish unless it affects delivery

**End of day**  
- Lock the cut again
- Send a status update if needed
- Set the client expectation that new requests after cutoff roll to the next version unless they’re true blockers

---

### Day 3 — QC, export, delivery buffer
**Morning**  
- Full QC watch-through
- Check picture, audio, titles, legal, timing
- Confirm no temp elements are left in the export path

**Midday**  
- Export final deliverables
- Spot-check the actual export
- Verify runtime, codec, frame rate, resolution, aspect
- Run checksum if required

**Afternoon**  
- Upload / deliver
- Verify the file opens and plays correctly
- Keep a buffer for re-export or upload failure

---

## Revision split

### Dangerous to ignore
Anything that can:
- fail QC
- break continuity
- cause a client rejection
- affect legal text
- affect sync, audio, or export spec

### Can wait
- polish
- preference notes
- micro-timing
- cosmetic cleanup
- non-essential visual tweaks

### Kick back for decision
- scope changes
- conflicting notes
- anything that overrides prior approval
- requests where they need to choose between two options

---

## Hard cutoff
**Day 1, 4:30 PM:** no new creative changes after this point.  
Only clarifications, logging, and prep.

If you want, I can also turn this into a **copy-paste work plan** with checkboxes for each block.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is usable. Still want one more layer though — the part that actually keeps the day from getting eaten alive.

What I’d want added is:
- a hard rule for what happens if a client note comes in after the cutoff
- a clean distinction between “can wait” and “not today, but maybe if the cut stays stable”
- one or two concrete examples in each bucket, because otherwise it’s still a little abstract

Also, Day 2 needs a bit more teeth. If I’m only doing must-do revisions, I want that spelled out as a no-polish pass — like don’t touch trims, don’t rework pacing unless it’s tied to a blocking note, don’t wander into cleanup just because I’m already there.

If you can tighten those parts and make the cutoff rule explicit, that’d make it actually field-useful.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter, field-useful version.

## 3-day plan

### Day 1 — freeze, triage, unblock
**9–11**  
- Freeze picture
- Duplicate the project / sequence
- Gather all notes in one place
- Mark the current version as the working baseline

**11–1**  
- Triage every note into:
  - **dangerous to ignore**
  - **can wait**
  - **kick back for decision**

**2–4**  
- Clear blockers only
- Ask the client about anything unclear or conflicting
- Do not start polish passes yet

**4:30 cutoff**  
- No more creative changes
- Only logging, prep, and note cleanup after this point

**End of day update**
> Hi [Client Name] — I’ve frozen the current version and sorted the remaining notes. I’m moving first on the items that affect delivery, and I have a few points that need your decision before I can finish the next pass cleanly. I’ll send those separately so I don’t guess on anything important.

---

### Day 2 — must-do revisions only
**Morning**  
- Work only on **dangerous to ignore** items
- No polish pass
- No trim tinkering
- No pacing rework unless it’s tied to a blocking note
- No cleanup just because you’re already in the section

**Afternoon**  
- Do one downstream pass on anything that changed
- Check titles, audio, timing, and any affected exports
- Stay out of cosmetic cleanup unless it affects delivery

**End of day**  
- Lock the cut again
- Send a status update if needed

---

### Day 3 — QC, export, delivery buffer
**Morning**  
- Full QC watch-through
- Check picture, audio, titles, legal, timing
- Confirm no temp elements are left in the export path

**Midday**  
- Export final deliverables
- Spot-check the actual export
- Verify runtime, codec, frame rate, resolution, aspect
- Run checksum if required

**Afternoon**  
- Upload / deliver
- Verify the file opens and plays correctly
- Keep a buffer for re-export or upload failure

---

## Revision buckets

### Dangerous to ignore
Anything that can:
- fail QC
- break continuity
- cause a client rejection
- affect legal text
- affect sync, audio, or export spec

**Examples**
- wrong legal line
- missing shot
- audio out of sync
- clipped dialogue
- export at the wrong frame rate

### Can wait
Things that are worth doing **if the cut stays stable**, but won’t block delivery.

**Examples**
- small trim cleanup
- minor pacing polish
- cosmetic title adjustment
- tiny audio level touch-up
- alternate wording that doesn’t change meaning

### Kick back for decision
Anything that changes scope, conflicts with prior approval, or needs the client to choose.

**Examples**
- “Use version A or version B?”
- “Replace this scene or keep it?”
- “Do you want the longer ending or the shorter one?”
- new request that wasn’t in the approved notes
- conflicting feedback from different people

---

## Hard cutoff rule
**After Day 1, 4:30 PM:**
- new client notes do **not** go into the current cut unless they are a true delivery blocker
- anything else gets logged for the next round
- if it’s unclear, it gets kicked back for a decision
- no silent scope creep

### What counts as a true delivery blocker
- QC failure
- missing approved content
- legal issue
- sync/audio issue
- wrong export spec
- something that would likely get the master rejected

### What does not
- polish
- preference changes
- micro-timing
- cosmetic cleanup
- “while you’re in there” requests

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page version with checkboxes and a tiny note log** so you can use it while you work.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. This is close enough to actually use, but I’d still want it one notch more operational.

Main thing: give me the note rule in plain language, not just categories.

Like:
- if it’s after cutoff and it’s not a blocker, it waits
- if it changes scope, it gets kicked back
- if it’s ambiguous, it doesn’t get guessed at

And I’d want the Day 2 no-polish rule stated almost like a guardrail, because that’s the part that saves the day:
- no trim polishing
- no pacing rework unless it fixes a blocker
- no cleanup pass just because the section is open
- no wandering into “small improvements”

The bucket examples are good. I’d just want one more example in the kick-back bucket that’s the kind of thing clients actually do — like, changing direction late or asking to choose between two versions after approval was already given.

So yeah, usable, but if you can tighten the cutoff rule into one blunt paragraph and make Day 2 sound a little less optional, that’d be better.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter version with the cutoff rule and Day 2 guardrail made blunt.

## 3-day plan

### Day 1 — freeze, triage, unblock
**9–11**  
- Freeze picture
- Duplicate the project / sequence
- Gather all notes in one place
- Mark the current version as the working baseline

**11–1**  
- Triage every note into:
  - **dangerous to ignore**
  - **can wait**
  - **kick back for decision**

**2–4**  
- Clear blockers only
- Ask the client about anything unclear or conflicting
- Do not start polish passes yet

**4:30 cutoff**  
- No more creative changes
- Only logging, prep, and note cleanup after this point

**End of day update**
> Hi [Client Name] — I’ve frozen the current version and sorted the remaining notes. I’m moving first on the items that affect delivery, and I have a few points that need your decision before I can finish the next pass cleanly. I’ll send those separately so I don’t guess on anything important.

---

### Day 2 — must-do revisions only
**Morning**  
- Work only on **dangerous to ignore** items
- No polish pass
- No trim polishing
- No pacing rework unless it fixes a blocker
- No cleanup just because the section is open
- No wandering into “small improvements”

**Afternoon**  
- Do one downstream pass on anything that changed
- Check titles, audio, timing, and any affected exports
- Stay out of cosmetic cleanup unless it affects delivery

**End of day**  
- Lock the cut again
- Send a status update if needed

---

### Day 3 — QC, export, delivery buffer
**Morning**  
- Full QC watch-through
- Check picture, audio, titles, legal, timing
- Confirm no temp elements are left in the export path

**Midday**  
- Export final deliverables
- Spot-check the actual export
- Verify runtime, codec, frame rate, resolution, aspect
- Run checksum if required

**Afternoon**  
- Upload / deliver
- Verify the file opens and plays correctly
- Keep a buffer for re-export or upload failure

---

## Revision buckets

### Dangerous to ignore
Anything that can:
- fail QC
- break continuity
- cause a client rejection
- affect legal text
- affect sync, audio, or export spec

**Examples**
- wrong legal line
- missing shot
- audio out of sync
- clipped dialogue
- export at the wrong frame rate

### Can wait
Things that are worth doing **if the cut stays stable**, but won’t block delivery.

**Examples**
- small trim cleanup
- minor pacing polish
- cosmetic title adjustment
- tiny audio level touch-up
- alternate wording that doesn’t change meaning

### Kick back for decision
Anything that changes scope, conflicts with prior approval, or needs the client to choose.

**Examples**
- “Use version A or version B?”
- “Replace this scene or keep it?”
- “Do you want the longer ending or the shorter one?”
- late direction change after approval
- “Can we switch back to the earlier version?” after the newer one was already approved
- new request that wasn’t in the approved notes
- conflicting feedback from different people

---

## Hard cutoff rule
After **Day 1, 4:30 PM**, the rule is simple:

- if it’s **not a blocker**, it waits
- if it **changes scope**, it gets kicked back
- if it’s **ambiguous**, don’t guess — ask
- if it’s a true delivery blocker, it gets handled
- everything else rolls to the next round

### What counts as a true delivery blocker
- QC failure
- missing approved content
- legal issue
- sync/audio issue
- wrong export spec
- something that would likely get the master rejected

### What does not
- polish
- preference changes
- micro-timing
- cosmetic cleanup
- “while you’re in there” requests

If you want, I can turn this into a **single copy-paste working sheet** with the schedule, cutoff rule, and note buckets all in one place.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the one. Usable.

The blunt cutoff rule is exactly what I needed — no blocker, it waits; scope change, kick it back; ambiguous, don’t guess. That’s the part that actually stops the day from disappearing.

And Day 2 finally reads like a hard no-polish pass, which is the real guardrail. Good.

I can work with this as-is.
